narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings—dreaming of law school and then facing this prediction of a 79% chance of not passing the bar exam feels like a gut punch, especially when you've poured so much into your journey. Our model looked at your profile and saw a story shaped mostly by your LSAT score of 30.5, which stood out as the strongest signal pulling toward the risk of failure, as it's on the lower side compared to what typically succeeds in rigorous programs like this. Right behind that was your Year 1 decile of 3.0, suggesting an early struggle to rank among the top performers in a competitive school environment, which amplified those concerns. Your Year 3 decile of 2.0 built on that pattern, painting a picture of grades that didn't climb as high as the model hoped for sustained bar prep success. Coming from a middle-income family added another layer, as it sometimes means fewer resources for the intense tutoring or support that can make a difference in these high-stakes exams. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.3, while solid, didn't quite counterbalance those trends, nudging the outlook further toward challenge. Factors like being a white female pursuing full-time studies wove in subtly but consistently in the same direction, reflecting patterns the model has seen in past outcomes. It's not that any one thing defines you—it's this combination that made the model cautious about your bar passage odds, leading to the tough call on your application. You've got real strengths here, like that UGPA showing persistence, and targeting law full-time speaks to your drive. This isn't the end of your story; many have turned similar profiles around with focused LSAT retakes, grade-boosting strategies, or extra bar prep. Keep pushing—you're closer than this one prediction suggests. With encouragement, [Your Name/Advisor]
